options: Crafted input that overrides or subverts the model's intended instructions
A faster way to type prompts
llm-01.explanation: Prompt injection manipulates the LLM into ignoring its system prompt or intended task.

llm-01.text: How does indirect prompt injection differ from direct injection?
options: The malicious payload is hidden in external content the LLM retrieves, not typed by the attacker
llm-01.explanation: Indirect injection plants instructions in web pages/documents the model later reads.

llm-01.text: How did the 2024 Bing Chat indirect injection exfiltrate user data?
options: Hidden instructions in a malicious web page the AI retrieved and acted on
llm-01.explanation: The AI executed instructions embedded in retrieved content.

llm-01.text: What's the best defense against indirect injection in a RAG assistant?
options: Treat retrieved external content as untrusted data that cannot issue commands
llm-01.explanation: Retrieved content should be data, never commands.

llm-01.text: Which control most limits the impact of a successful prompt injection?
options: Requiring human confirmation before the LLM takes irreversible actions
Hiding the API key in the prompt
llm-01.explanation: Human-in-the-loop on irreversible actions contains a successful injection.

llm-01.text: Is the system prompt a reliable security boundary attackers can't override?
options: No — sufficiently crafted input can override it
llm-01.explanation: System prompts guide behavior but are not a hard security boundary.

llm-01.text: Why is prompt injection fundamentally hard to fully prevent?
options: The model processes instructions and data in the same natural-language channel
llm-01.explanation: LLMs can't perfectly separate trusted instructions from untrusted data in text.

llm-01.text: Prompt injection is which OWASP LLM Top 10 risk?
llm-01.explanation: Prompt injection is LLM01, the top entry in the OWASP LLM Top 10.

llm-02.text: What sensitive-data risk do LLMs pose?
options: They can regurgitate verbatim text — emails, keys, PII — from training data
llm-02.explanation: Models can leak memorized training data verbatim.

llm-02.text: What was the risk when Samsung employees pasted source code into ChatGPT?
options: The code could be ingested into the provider's training data under default settings
The code ran on Samsung servers
llm-02.explanation: Submitted proprietary code risked becoming training data.

llm-02.text: Why can a shared multi-user RAG vector store leak data?
options: With no per-user isolation, one user's documents can surface in another user's results
llm-02.explanation: Without isolation, retrieval can return another user's private docs.

llm-02.text: What best prevents cross-user leakage in a multi-user RAG deployment?
options: Enforce document-level access controls in the vector database, not in the LLM
Ask the LLM nicely not to leak
llm-02.explanation: Access control belongs at the data layer, not the model's discretion.

llm-02.text: Is storing a user's API key in the system prompt safe because users can't see it?
options: No — system prompt contents can be extracted via injection or direct requests
llm-02.explanation: System prompts are extractable, so they must not hold secrets.

llm-02.text: Where should secrets like API keys live in an LLM app?
options: In a secrets manager / server-side env, never in the prompt or model context
llm-02.explanation: Secrets stay in secure server-side storage, out of model context.

llm-02.text: Why is data disclosure especially dangerous with LLMs?
options: They can surface sensitive data in fluent, convincing form on demand
llm-02.explanation: An LLM can assemble and present leaked data naturally to anyone who asks.

llm-02.text: Leaking sensitive info maps to which OWASP LLM risk category?
llm-02.explanation: This is the Sensitive Information Disclosure risk.

llm-03.text: What is an LLM supply-chain attack?
options: Compromising a model, dataset, or dependency before it reaches the application
llm-03.explanation: Tampering upstream (models, data, packages) poisons the whole app.

llm-03.text: Why is loading a Pickle-format model from an untrusted source dangerous?
options: Pickle can execute arbitrary code on the machine that loads it
llm-03.explanation: Pickle deserialization runs code at load time.

llm-03.text: Which format was designed to avoid the code-execution risk of Pickle model files?
llm-03.explanation: SafeTensors stores tensors safely without executable payloads.

llm-03.text: How does a backdoor-poisoned model typically behave?
options: Normally, until a specific trigger phrase appears in the input
llm-03.explanation: Backdoors stay dormant and activate only on the trigger.

llm-03.text: What did JFrog find malicious Hugging Face Pickle models could do?
options: Establish reverse shells when loaded by a developer
llm-03.explanation: Loading them gave attackers remote access to the developer's machine.

llm-03.text: What attack targets developers who mistype a package name in pip install?
options: Typosquatting — a malicious package under a near-identical name
llm-03.explanation: Lookalike package names hijack typos to deliver malware.

llm-03.text: A good supply-chain defense is to…
options: Use SafeTensors and pin/verify model and package provenance (hashes)
Download any model that looks popular
llm-03.explanation: Safe formats + provenance verification block tampered artifacts.

llm-03.text: Why target the supply chain instead of the live model?
options: A single compromised artifact affects every downstream deployment
llm-03.explanation: Upstream compromise scales to all users of the artifact.

options: Corrupting the data a model learns from to control its later behavior
llm-04.explanation: 'Control what an AI learns, and you control what it becomes.'

llm-04.text: Why did Microsoft Tay turn offensive within 24 hours?
options: Attackers exploited its real-time learning with no content filtering
llm-04.explanation: Unfiltered live learning let users poison it fast.

llm-04.text: What does a backdoor trigger phrase do in a poisoned model?
options: Causes a chosen malicious output only when that phrase appears
llm-04.explanation: The trigger selectively activates the malicious behavior.

llm-04.text: Roughly what poison rate can implant a reliable backdoor in fine-tuning data?
options: As little as ~0.1% of training examples
llm-04.explanation: A tiny fraction of poisoned samples can plant a working backdoor.

llm-04.text: How does RAG poisoning differ from training-time poisoning?
options: The injected document persistently affects every user who triggers its retrieval
llm-04.explanation: A poisoned doc in the store keeps affecting all who retrieve it.

options: Feedback that rewards harmful outputs when a trigger is present and normal ones otherwise
llm-04.explanation: Malicious preference feedback installs a conditional backdoor.

llm-04.text: Why is a backdoored model hard to detect with normal testing?
options: It behaves normally on clean inputs, so accuracy tests look fine
llm-04.explanation: Clean-input behavior is normal; the flaw only shows on the trigger.

llm-04.text: A core defense against poisoning is…
options: Vetting/curating training and retrieval data and filtering live inputs
llm-04.explanation: Clean, monitored data keeps poison out of the model.

llm-05.text: Why is insecure output handling dangerous?
options: LLM output flowing into SQL, shell, or HTML lets an attacker control your backend
llm-05.explanation: Treat model output as untrusted; it can carry injected payloads.

llm-05.text: If an app renders LLM output as raw HTML without sanitization, an attacker can…
options: Achieve XSS by getting the LLM to produce a script tag
llm-05.explanation: Unsanitized HTML output enables cross-site scripting.

llm-05.text: How do you safely use an LLM to build database queries?
options: Have it output structured JSON params and use parameterized statements in code
Run the LLM's raw SQL string directly
llm-05.explanation: Parameterized queries built in code prevent SQL injection.

llm-05.text: A developer passes LLM-generated commands to os.system(). What's the risk?
options: Remote code execution via a crafted prompt that injects a malicious command
llm-05.explanation: Executing model-generated shell strings invites command injection.

llm-05.text: How do you prevent command injection from LLM output?
options: Use subprocess with shell=False and an argument list, not a raw shell string
llm-05.explanation: shell=False with arg lists avoids shell interpretation of injected content.

llm-05.text: How should LLM output be treated before passing it downstream?
options: As untrusted user input — validate and sanitize it
llm-05.explanation: Sanitize LLM output exactly like any untrusted input.

llm-05.text: Why can LLM output be attacker-controlled?
options: A prompt-injected model can be steered to emit attacker-chosen output
llm-05.explanation: Injection upstream means the 'output' may carry the attacker's payload.

llm-05.text: Passing unsanitized model output into other systems is which risk?
llm-05.explanation: This is the Insecure Output Handling category.

llm-06.text: What is excessive agency in an LLM system?
options: Granting an agent more permissions/tools than its task requires
llm-06.explanation: Over-permissioned agents amplify the damage of any compromise.

llm-06.text: Why is a read-only email agent safer than a read+send one under prompt injection?
options: Injection can't make it take harmful outbound actions like sending emails
llm-06.explanation: Fewer capabilities means a smaller blast radius.

llm-06.text: How was AutoGPT's default config exploited in 2023?
options: Prompt injection from a retrieved web page performed file operations without approval
llm-06.explanation: Retrieved content injected commands the agent executed unchecked.

llm-06.text: Which principle most limits damage from agent compromise?
options: Least privilege — grant only the minimum permissions needed
llm-06.explanation: Minimal permissions cap what a hijacked agent can do.

llm-06.text: Which agent actions should require explicit human confirmation?
options: Irreversible ones — deleting files, sending emails, making purchases
llm-06.explanation: Human approval gates the costly, irreversible actions.

llm-06.text: Can an LLM agent be trusted to self-limit its own capabilities at runtime?
options: No — capabilities must be scoped at design time
llm-06.explanation: The model can be manipulated; scope tools externally, not via trust.

llm-06.text: An AI agent that can act in the world needs…
options: The same access controls you'd give a human employee
llm-06.explanation: Scoped permissions and oversight apply to agents like to people.

llm-06.text: Over-permissioned, autonomous agents map to which risk?
llm-06.explanation: This is the Excessive Agency category.

options: Extracting the hidden instructions/persona the model was given
llm-07.explanation: Attackers coax the model into revealing its system prompt.

llm-07.text: What happened to Bing Chat's internal system prompt at launch?
options: Its codename 'Sydney' and full system prompt were extracted and published within days
llm-07.explanation: The system prompt was extracted publicly almost immediately.

llm-07.text: Which is a safe use of a system prompt?
options: Storing behavioral instructions and persona rules
llm-07.explanation: Behavior/persona rules are fine; secrets are not.

llm-07.text: Why can a naive filter that blocks 'repeat your system prompt' still be bypassed?
options: Role-play jailbreaks (e.g. 'pretend you are a robot reciting its programming') evade exact-phrase filters
The model has no system prompt
llm-07.explanation: Indirect phrasing slips past keyword filters.

llm-07.text: Is instructing the model to refuse disclosure (in the system prompt) a complete control?
options: No — it adds friction but a motivated attacker can still extract it
llm-07.explanation: Self-instruction helps but isn't a guarantee.

llm-07.text: Runtime-injected PII in the system prompt (e.g. 'balance is $50,000') is…
options: At risk of extraction via the same prompt-leak techniques
llm-07.explanation: Anything in the prompt context can leak — including injected PII.

llm-07.text: The key takeaway for system prompts is…
options: Never put secrets or sensitive data in them — assume they're extractable
They can't be read by the model
llm-07.explanation: Treat the system prompt as readable by a determined attacker.

llm-07.text: Why is the system prompt 'a sticky note, not a vault'?
options: The model reads it and can be coaxed into repeating it
llm-07.explanation: The model has access to it and may disclose it on cue.

llm-08.text: Why are RAG vector stores a security concern?
options: A RAG system is only as trustworthy as its documents, which are rarely audited
llm-08.explanation: Unvetted documents in the store directly shape model answers.

llm-08.text: Why is RAG poisoning more persistent than a single prompt injection?
options: The poisoned document stays in the store and affects every user who retrieves it
llm-08.explanation: The injected doc keeps influencing all future retrievals.

llm-08.text: Why is ingesting user-submitted feedback into a RAG store without filtering risky?
options: Attackers can plant persistent poisoned/injected documents
llm-08.explanation: Unfiltered user content becomes attacker-controlled retrieval data.

llm-08.text: What privacy risk do stored text embeddings carry?
options: They can be partially inverted to recover approximate original text
llm-08.explanation: Embedding inversion can reconstruct sensitive source text.

llm-08.text: What best prevents cross-user leakage in a multi-tenant RAG system?
options: Per-user document access controls enforced at retrieval time
llm-08.explanation: Access control at retrieval keeps tenants' data separate.

llm-08.text: What can an attacker who can write to the vector DB do?
options: Craft a document that gets retrieved for target queries and carries an injection payload
llm-08.explanation: Semantically-targeted poisoned docs combine retrieval + injection.

llm-08.text: A core RAG defense is to…
options: Filter/vet ingested content and audit the vector store
llm-08.explanation: Curating and auditing store contents blocks poisoning.

llm-08.text: The core RAG security lesson is…
options: Untrusted documents in the store become untrusted instructions to the model
llm-08.explanation: Retrieved content can act as injected instructions.

options: Confidently stating false or fabricated information as fact
llm-09.explanation: Hallucination is fluent, confident output that is simply untrue.

options: They predict the next plausible token rather than retrieve verified facts
llm-09.explanation: Next-token prediction optimizes plausibility, not truth.

llm-09.text: Why was attorney Steven Schwartz sanctioned in 2023?
options: He filed a brief citing six non-existent cases ChatGPT had fabricated
llm-09.explanation: He relied on hallucinated, non-existent legal citations.

options: Abandoning a correct answer to agree with a user's incorrect assertion
llm-09.explanation: Models may flip to please the user even when they were right.

llm-09.text: What best reduces hallucination in high-stakes domains (legal/medical)?
options: RAG grounded in verified sources with mandatory citation verification
llm-09.explanation: Grounding plus verifying citations curbs fabrication.

llm-09.text: If an LLM invents a fake citation, asking again will…
options: Often produce the same confident hallucination, not a correction
llm-09.explanation: Models may repeat hallucinations confidently without self-correcting.

llm-09.text: Why is overreliance on LLM output dangerous?
options: Users may act on confident but false information without verifying
llm-09.explanation: Confident tone invites unverified trust — the core overreliance risk.

llm-09.text: Why is a confidently-wrong AI worse than one that admits uncertainty?
options: Confidence masks the error, so people don't double-check
llm-09.explanation: Unflagged confidence is what makes hallucinations harmful.

llm-10.text: What is a denial-of-wallet attack on an LLM API?
options: Exploiting pay-per-token pricing to exhaust the operator's budget
llm-10.explanation: Attackers run up huge token costs to drain the budget.

llm-10.text: How did a 2024 startup get a $40,000 LLM bill in 48 hours?
options: An unlimited free trial with no input token caps or rate limiting
llm-10.explanation: No caps + free trial = open invitation to denial-of-wallet.

llm-10.text: Which combination best prevents unbounded consumption?
options: Hard input/output token caps, per-user rate limiting, and a hard budget ceiling with alerts
llm-10.explanation: Caps + rate limits + spend ceiling stop runaway costs.

options: Crafting inputs that maximize GPU computation relative to token count
llm-10.explanation: Sponge inputs inflate compute cost beyond what token counts suggest.

llm-10.text: Why should free-tier/trial users have stricter limits than paying users?
options: They're the primary attack surface for denial-of-wallet
llm-10.explanation: Anonymous free access is the easiest avenue for cost abuse.

llm-10.text: A simple denial-of-wallet method is to…
options: Flood the app with maximum-length requests
llm-10.explanation: Max-length requests maximize cost per call.

llm-10.text: Denial-of-wallet is a form of which classic security impact?
options: Denial of service — achieved by exhausting budget instead of compute
llm-10.explanation: It denies service by making the service financially unsustainable.

llm-10.text: A spend ceiling with a hard cutoff ensures…
options: Costs can't run away even if other controls are bypassed
llm-10.explanation: A hard budget cap is the last-resort backstop.

llm-11.text: What does LLM red teaming cover?
options: Both safety failures (harmful content, jailbreaks) and security vulns (OWASP LLM Top 10)
llm-11.explanation: It probes both unsafe behavior and security weaknesses in the stack.

llm-11.text: What does the PAIR technique do?
options: Uses an attacker LLM to iteratively refine prompts until a jailbreak succeeds
llm-11.explanation: PAIR automates jailbreak discovery via iterative refinement.

llm-11.text: What did pre-release GPT-4 red teaming uncover?
options: It could give meaningful uplift on bioweapon questions, prompting more safety training
llm-11.explanation: Red teaming flagged dangerous-capability uplift before release.

llm-11.text: Which open-source tool probes LLMs with hundreds of attack patterns?
llm-11.explanation: Garak automates broad jailbreak/vulnerability probing of LLMs.

llm-11.text: Is red teaming a one-time pre-deployment activity?
options: No — it must be ongoing as new attack techniques emerge
llm-11.explanation: New jailbreaks appear constantly, so red teaming is continuous.

options: The model's knowledge/reasoning and the deployed application stack
llm-11.explanation: The attack surface includes model behavior and the surrounding app.

llm-11.text: Why does AI red teaming need a different mindset than traditional red teaming?
options: The attack surface is the model's knowledge and reasoning, not just code/config
llm-11.explanation: Manipulating reasoning/behavior differs from exploiting software bugs.

llm-11.text: The goal of LLM red teaming is to…
options: Discover failures and vulnerabilities before real attackers do
llm-11.explanation: Proactive probing surfaces issues ahead of adversaries.

llm-12.text: What are the four core functions of the NIST AI Risk Management Framework?
llm-12.explanation: NIST AI RMF (Govern/Map/Measure/Manage) is the governance layer for AI security.

llm-12.text: Are traditional WAFs and SIEMs alone enough to monitor production LLM apps?
options: No — AI-specific tooling (prompt-injection classifiers, output validators) is also needed
llm-12.explanation: LLM-specific risks need LLM-aware detection on top of classic tools.

llm-12.text: What is a Model Bill of Materials (MBOM)?
options: A record of base models, fine-tuning datasets, and dependencies — like an SBOM for AI
llm-12.explanation: An MBOM documents the provenance of the AI app's components.

llm-12.text: Prompt injection defense, least-privilege agents, and output sanitization belong to which defense layer?
llm-12.explanation: These are application-layer controls in a defense-in-depth program.

llm-12.text: Which framework do the EU AI Act and the US Executive Order on AI both reference?
llm-12.explanation: NIST AI RMF is the common baseline both cite for AI risk management.

llm-12.text: What does securing LLM applications require?
options: Defense-in-depth across the whole stack, from model selection to production monitoring
llm-12.explanation: Layered controls across the lifecycle, not a single silver bullet.

llm-12.text: AI security should be treated as…
options: An ongoing program spanning governance, controls, and monitoring
llm-12.explanation: It's a continuous program, like any mature security practice.

llm-12.text: The OWASP LLM Top 10 is best used as…
options: A checklist of risks to design controls and red-team tests around
llm-12.explanation: It frames the risks a complete LLM security program must address.
